posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
I’ve been excited to try some 2021s from Beaujolais since the talk that it’s more of a classic vintage than we’ve seen in a while. Please weigh in if you have general thoughts about that, whether it’s true or not. So far I’ve only tried one wine: Thivin Côte de Brouilly. If this wine is any indicator, I’ll be looking forward to trying and buying from some of my other favorite producers. It’s been a few days, so no detailed note, but I do remember a good bit of tartness in the well delineated red and black fruits, great refreshment factor, and a distinctive earth component that I usually get in this bottling, something reminiscent of Aglianico for some reason. There was nothing at all that I would call Rhône-like. Overall, I loved the wine. Wanting to drink more over the next year or two, with enough left over to age, I immediately bought more.

What 2021s have you tried? Any thoughts on the vintage in general? I know it’s early in terms of what’s available in the US at this point.

His summary was pretty broad and didn’t describe or recommend significant vs. minor increases. He also didn’t dissect the differences between producers who, at least in the US, come across as more expensive-ish vs. those in the ~$18-25 realm. 2020 Coudert Clos de la Roillette Fleurie was $19/bottle at my local, not known for best-in-nation prices. You can’t keep making a wine like that during record inflation without changing some things you do or adjusting prices moving forward.

Kris_Patten:

Beaujolais has to be careful, unlike Burgundy, consumers will walk away at a certain point, as has happened many times in the region due to price vs. quality.

This is absolutely a risk, but if quality rises commensurate with prices increases, the producers won’t care if the old guard stop buying. If quality is maintained, but the prices reflect added costs and inflation, the market will change very little.

A burger joint can’t keep charging $10 per burger for a decade without sacrificing a lot in terms of quality (of product and of life). People’s expectations evolve, though, and even the same quality product at a cut rate price can eventually lose favor. Cru Beaujolais could lose consumers with prices increases, but they can also lose consumers with degradation in quality and inability to broadly adopt desirable farming methods. We already see many younger wine drinkers looking for the farming practices in a wine before they even ask about the taste. Very few products that are extremely well-known for tremendous value maintain the equilibrium for very long before something upsets the balance. My take is that there is concern that the balance cannot be maintained given entry level Cru Bojo can be purchased in the US for cheaper than Meiomi Red Blend.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Bulk price for 100 liters of Chiroubles is around 200 EUR; for good quality Moulin-a-Vent, maybe 450. If the crus can be had for 200 EUR per hectoliter, what do you think that implies for the pricing of Beaujolais-Villages, and in turn generic Beaujolais? By contrast, for context, 100 liters of Bourgogne Côte d’Or Pinot Noir trades for around 660 EUR.

The best vineyards of the Beaujolais are often seriously sloping, prone to erosion, and difficult to mechanize due to goblet pruning and quite high planting densities. Yet they are less well valorized than the least prestigious AOCs from the Côte d’Or (which happen to be, in many cases, much easier to cultivate).

This is a problem for the region’s producers, and by extension for anyone who wants to see the Beaujolais realize all its potential.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Otto_Forsberg:

So if they know how to handle hot vintages now, based on my experiences, I’m not looking forward to any future warm vintages…

The knowledge is there to learn and implement. That takes money. Not everyone has the mindset for the dynamic thinking to deal with the more varied weather well. That’s a lot different than just doing what grandpa did and maybe adopting some new fad. Is there incentive to take the time to learn more? Money and space for extra equipment? Money for and availability of sufficient workforce in a more demanding vintage?

I just handled some top Pinot Noir in this hot vintage. The canopy management was clearly spot-on. The pick decisions were right. Was there any raisins or sunburn? Yes. Can you taste that shit if it gets in the wine? Yes. Did we take our time and sort it all out? Yes. Think 5-10% of the volume sorted out, when with most vintages for these sites it would be under 1%.

Raisins represent a bunch of sugar (which you’re guessing the volume of) on top of the nasty taste. If you have a range of ripeness in clusters, like with head trained vines, guess what? You can see the range of ripeness. You can sort out the “perfectly fine” “Napa-ripe” clusters. Getting those out means a lower ABV and less “ripe” character.

Of course there are realities like a heat wave making everybody want to pick at the same time without the available labor to fully do that. Big players may have their own crews that they can usually loan out to others in a normal vintage. They also have clout and more work to be had. So, a challenging vintage may have “haves” and “have nots” due to both resources and capability.

It’s important for critics to call it as it is. Why should we consumers blindly avoid an excellent wine because we “know better”? Then we’re punishing someone for doing a good job - the type of effort we’re asking the rest to put forward. We want to incentivize good wines and call out the bad or mediocre. If some random consumers sees a good rating with an appealing review for a wine from some unfamiliar region then don’t like the wine, they end up with a negative impression of that region and move on.

I think David’s point is a fair one, and even if he sometimes states things in a slightly irritating/surly way, I value his comment. I don’t think he’s trolling, he’s trying to defend his point and make his opinion. And let those without ever having made a hypocritical statement cast the first rock.

Back on subject, I’ve had many disappointing 2018s from normally solid providers (Coudert, Lapierre Diochon, Chermette, etc). But I’ve also had amazingly good wines. The Desvignes Morgon Javenieres blew me away - yes, bigger than 16 or 17, but taut, tensile, and super fresh. And JP Brun produced excellent wines (all 12.5% alc IIRC) across the board. It’s unfortunate that Roilette and Lapierre didn’t produce wines worthy of adulation, but if you write off every wine due to the scary 2018 reputation, you’re missing some really good wines too.

I certainly wont debate the possibility that some very good wines are produced in 2018. But living down in Florida, where you just don’t find these kinds of wines, you and va have to order them from places like New York City. I’m not taking flyers on wines from vintage that I know to be over-ripe. Made that mistake before. I will sample a few and then move on. It is too easy to find other vintages that satisfy greatly.

Robert, I think that’s exactly right.

I wish people would stop with the cynical argument that a vintage can’t be bad if there are any good wines made in it. The people making that argument are usually in the business of selling wine or are trolling. If there’s a hurricane at noon, it’s a day of terrible weather despite a sunny fifteen minutes at 6:00 am.

There’s a big difference between perceived “weak” vintages and bad vintages. I’m a big fan of vintage variation and buy my favorite producers in almost all vintages. But when vintages are bad (as opposed to weak), consumers have a perfectly valid choice not to buy the wines. There’s no enjoyment of vintage variation when the wine is just bad.

I will say, to your point, I often like the “challenging” vintages - which I think the critics refer to as the less ripe vintages - over the much more vaunted “ripe” vintages.

Yikes. Hard to know where to start. First, I don’t use broad euphemistic terms to refer to specific things (except in humor). For example, my prior post started off contesting the idea a raisin character is just one of those things you get in a warm vintage. No, raising character comes from raisins and raisinating fruit. You can see, feel and taste that in the berries, and you can sort that out. I added sunburned grapes, which impart a taste, and shriveling berries, where much of the “ripe” (over-ripe) taste comes from. In other words, with the challenge of a warm vintage like this one I’m dealing with right now, you can often make a normally ripe wine. As far as you can predict the challenges of a vintage, you can address that to a large degree in the vineyard.

I don’t give a crap about the obsolete dinosaur critics who designated warm years and big wines as inherently better. Some of these cooler vintages have taken away the option of late picking, often forcing them to make a good wine for a change. Well, often it has been a learning moment and inspired changes.

I’m challenging a framework of thought. Again, if the good producers make good wine in a challenging vintage, the ones who didn’t meet the challenge failed. But, it was self-evident it wasn’t a general challenge that couldn’t be met. Blaming the vintage seems to be off the mark. And, again, red flag the vintage and make your choices.

And again, the context here is global warming and Beaujolais. I’m still baffled that someone would read in “cool vintage” when I’m talking about vintage challenges that can be met.

Anyway, again, I’m partially riffing on what William said about market price and perception and the capability of producers to improve quality. You can’t do the sort of quality control I’m talking about at crazy cheap prices. The prevalence of those more passively made wines effects the perception of the region. So, there is a dilemma. That’s not going to change overnight. But, recognizing and rewarding the better wines grows market awareness. We see quite a range of price within a Cru. So, there is room for more producers to improve quality to the point they can double or triple what they charge over a decade or so.

posts.cooked: I am hesitant to revive this thread, but I’d like to mention that I’ve now tried a second 2021: JP Brun Morgon. Crunchy, tart fruits, more red fruited than the Thivin, and very floral. Just beautifully aromatic. Nice, brisk acidity. The palate was significantly less giving than the nose, but still showed a classic style that I’ve been missing. posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Today: 2021 Chermette Fleurie Poncié. Tart red fruit, a subtle VA lift that’s complimenting the floral notes, something like tobacco, and potpourri. It’s relatively light on the palate and super refreshing. It does lack the aromatic complexity of the Brun, and it’s a bit thin on the palate, but it’s delicious. My only real complaint is the plastic cork! It doesn’t even state the maker, so I am guessing it isn’t one of the high quality ones. No problem drinking what I have over the next couple of years, but I have no idea why anyone still uses these on high quality wines.

Overall, Brun and Thivin set a high standard that this wine isn’t living up to, but it still like the style I’m seeing here, which reminds me of the cooler vintages that we haven’t seen much in the past few years. Hopefully this fleshes out a bit over the next few months.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Now I’ve got 2021 Clos de la Roilette Cuvee Tardive open. Predictably, this is the least open for business of what I’ve had so far. It is quite complex, with brambly, crunchy red and black fruits, graphite (whatever that means), some soil notes, and a hint of green herbal character. The fruit on this is less forward than on the others. The palate is refreshing (thank goodness), but a bit clenched. There’s plenty of concentration that is in no way overbearing. This is the most obvious in having more potential than immediate pleasure. No surprise. The rest of my bottles will get tucked away for at least 5 years before I revisit. 10 is probably better, then drinking over quite a few years after that. This is definitely not the best Cuvee Tardive I’ve had, but I like it a lot better than I have some of the recent, really ripe vintages. Very glad to have more.

There are a few more I plan to try that haven’t hit the US yet (maybe not even released yet, at least in one case). I’ll try to remember to revive the thread if/when I’m able to get my hands on them. This all makes me wish I’d bought a lot more 2011 and 2014 when I had the chance, but I am grateful for a vintage like this that I’m finding fun to drink, at least when focusing on producers I like and regard highly.

Just got back from a 2021 Beaujolais tasting at a wine shop in Indy. There were several good ones, but these three stood out:

Edit: the Lafont is the 2020 vintage and still a QPR.
2021

2020 Jean-Marc Lafont Cote du Puy Morgon - the wine rep told me Lafont bought the juice from a family that decided to get out of the biz. It that true? I don’t know; I don’t care. This wine was incredible! HUGE nose and great depth. Retailed at $18.99 and a no brainer case buy at that price, even moreso at the absurd $15 sale price. Could be a house wine all winter. Drink through 2027-2030 easy. Seek it out!

2021 Thevenet “Vielles Vignes” Morgon - Another holy cow wine. Very zippy and nice minerals, but the depth is there. This wine is low- or no-sulphur added. At $30 on sale, I bought a half case to test how long it can go.

2021 Chanrion Cote de Brouilly - less heat in this vintage made this a winner. Very good QPR around $21 on sale. I bought 8 bottles to try over the next 5-7 years.

Other interesting wines:
2021 Thevenet Regnie “En Voiture Simone” - floral, interesting.
2021 Dom. Subpar Moulin-a-Vent En Brenay - meaty

Overall, none of the 2021s were hot or alcoholic. I think this is a good vintage for my palate.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked:
Ok, just to clarify:

My understanding of “natural winemaking” (using this phrase as a term of art as used in wine boards/wine journalism) is winemaking that follows the wine making philosophy of Jules Chauvet as interpreted by Jacques Neauport: semi-carbonic/carbonic maceration and low/no sulfur.

“Natural winemaking” is more complicated that (not going into the farming aspect or other nuances), but those two things are big components of “natural winemaking.”

My understanding is that, traditionally, in Beaujolais (where Jules Chauvet practiced his winemaking), semi-carbonic maceration is the “traditional” way to make wine. Jacques Neauport later took Chauvet’s understanding of traditional winemaking in Beaujolais and introduced it to other regions, like the Loire and Rhone, where semi-carbonic was not very traditional.

So, in Beaujolais, even though Foillard and Lapierre would be considered “natural” under the Chauvet/Neauport model, they are still practicing “traditional” winemaking in the Beaujolais sense.

I could be wrong, but people who know more should chime in.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Is there a statute of limitations on being a natural winemaker?

To link the spectrum with winemaking practices:

techno/industrial: thermovinification

Burgundian/aspirational: destemming, punchdowns, new oak

traditional/artisanal: whole bunch but crushed, maturation in cement, foudre or used barrels, sulfites at crush, after malo, and before bottling

natty: true carbonic maceration (i.e. tank gassed with CO2 at cuvaison, bottom valve left open to drain off any liberated juice), zero sulfites with the exception of a gram or two at bottling

While the latter approach was a great way to get attention to a rather moribund region, and while working with low sulfites with Gamay does make a certain about of sense as sulfites really tend to dry the wine out with this variety vs others I have experienced in vilification and élevage, it cannot be said that the last string of extreme vintages we’ve had have really favored this approach, and I really have an increasingly hard time with the acetate and brett (Gamay has a lot of precursors for volatile phenols, unfortunately) that often comes with it, to say nothing of mouse.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: I agree, and I wrote a rather polemical piece about this in Noble Rot a few years ago, getting into how the meaning of the term has changed over time, the various key actors and their intentions, and so forth. Like any aesthetic movement, I think it makes much more sense defining it historically, by looking at what people who call themselves natural winemakers have been doing over the years and who they were, and what sort of milieu they were operating in, rather than trying to come up with a “definition” based on sulfites or whatever; indeed, such attempts seem to me much like trying to define pointillism by dots per square inch or Cubism with the aid of a protractor. The assumption that natural winemakers are farming “naturally”, whatever that would mean, also betrays how naïve some observers are about what at least some of these producers are actually doing.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
You don’t need Chauvet to tell you that sulfur affects yeast population development: that’s sort of the whole point of using it! But I’m talking about the effect of sulfites added during élevage and before bottling on how wines’ tannins are perceived on the palate. So, at least hopefully, this is after the phase were active yeast populations are present in the wine.

I honestly don’t have a theory, it’s just an empirical observation having made a bit of Gamay in the last couple of vintages. But it is one that quite a few winemakers have confirmed.

posts.name: Yule Kim
posts.cooked: Very interesting information. Thank you for the follow up!

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I realize I only answered the first part of your question. Why do extreme vintages make it harder to make viable “natural wines”?

  • higher sugars = harder for yeasts to ferment to dryness
  • stressed vines produce musts deficient in nitrogen and other nutrients necessary for healthy complete fermentation
  • higher fruit temperatures at pick make fermentations harder to manage and increase the risk of early spoilage
  • higher must and wine pHs reduce the efficacy of sulfites and exercise less of an inhibiting effect on undesirable microflora
  • extreme temperatures in the vineyards modify the grape skin microbiome, favoring less desirable yeast and bacteria strains (at least based on what we have seen on recent vintages - I don’t know if this has been studied)

Combined with a desire not to use sulfites or to only use them at the end (when it’s too late) on the one hand, and an unwillingness to protect the wine microbiologically by inoculation, the results can be disastrous. In 2021, for my own amusement, I successfully (for now!) made a barrel of Moulin-à-Vent with only 20 ppm total sulfites, bottled after 18 months in neutral wood. In 2022, that simply wouldn’t have been possible.
